region_of_origin: East Asia (China, Japan); widely used in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for garland chrysanthemum as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this vegetable is absent in publicly available releases.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw garland chrysanthemum is approximately 100 g (about 1 cup of loosely packed leaves), commonly used as a vegetable in hotpot, stir-fries, or salads.

nutrient_source.title: Garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho), raw — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho), raw. Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chrysanthemum greens, raw (FDC ID 169230) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Vegetables — analogous estimate based on spinach and similar leafy greens (GI ≈ 15) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy greens (spinach, lettuce, watercress) which consistently test at GI 10–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (15 × 3.02) / 100 = 0.45, rounded to 0.5. This is a very low GL consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium-to-sodium ratio (567 mg potassium vs 118 mg sodium per 100 g; ratio >4:1) supports blood pressure regulation via DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Garland chrysanthemum is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its very high potassium content (567 mg/100 g) relative to sodium (118 mg/100 g); prepare without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve the net blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate content (3.02 g/100 g) with equal dietary fibre (3.0 g/100 g) yields negligible glycaemic impact and supports postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3 g carbohydrate and 3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, garland chrysanthemum has a negligible glycaemic load (GL ≈ 0.5 per serving) and is an excellent low-carbohydrate leafy vegetable choice for Type 2 Diabetes meal planning; avoid pairing with high-sugar sauces or oyster sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.09 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, high dietary fibre, and significant folate (79 mcg DFE/100 g) collectively support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Garland chrysanthemum is cardioprotective as a raw leafy green: its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and folate content support endothelial function and homocysteine regulation; include regularly in a heart-healthy diet and avoid cooking with high-sodium or high-fat sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Provides dietary fibre, calcium, iron, and vitamin C which support immune modulation and bone health, but lacks direct omega-3 or established anti-inflammatory polyphenol content sufficient to classify as anti-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Garland chrysanthemum may be included in an anti-inflammatory diet pattern for rheumatoid arthritis due to its calcium (117 mg/100 g), iron (2.29 mg/100 g), vitamin C (27.8 mg/100 g), and fibre content, but it does not independently qualify as an anti-inflammatory food; avoid high-sodium broths when cooking.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho; 茼蒿; Glebionis coronaria) is a leafy green vegetable widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore, Hong Kong, China, Japan, and Korea. The raw leaves are bright green, serrated, and tender with a distinctive mildly bitter, slightly aromatic flavour reminiscent of chrysanthemum flowers. It is most commonly used in hotpot (steamboat), stir-fries, soups, and blanched vegetable dishes. Nutritionally, raw garland chrysanthemum is a low-calorie, nutrient-dense vegetable providing only 24 kcal per 100 g. It is notably high in protein for a leafy green (3.36 g/100 g), contains 3.02 g carbohydrate and 3.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g, yielding a negligible glycaemic load. It is an excellent source of potassium (567 mg/100 g), calcium (117 mg/100 g), iron (2.29 mg/100 g), folate (79 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin C (27.8 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (152 mcg RAE/100 g). Sodium is moderate at 118 mg/100 g but the potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeds 4:1, making it suitable for DASH diet and hypertension management. The vegetable is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low net carbohydrate and high fibre content. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly known as 'tong ho' or 'tang ho' and in Japan as 'shungiku' (春菊).
common_user_queries: Is tang ho good for diabetes?
Is tong ho suitable for hypertension?
What is 茼蒿 in English?
Can I eat tang ho raw?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). Secondary micronutrients (saturated fat, fatty acid profile, vitamins, minerals) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chrysanthemum greens, raw (FDC ID 169230), which is the same species and preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using comparable non-starchy leafy greens. Overall provenance is strong and multi-source corroborated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.09 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.27 g), trans fat (0.0 g), sugar (0.9 g), vitamin C (27.8 mg), vitamin A (152 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, folate (79 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.0 g), vitamin E (0.89 mg), phosphorus (57 mg), magnesium (20 mg), zinc (0.45 mg), selenium (0.9 mcg), and water (91.5 g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 169230 (chrysanthemum greens, raw), as HPB Foodo does not report these fields. 2. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg and vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg: confirmed zero — this is a plant food that naturally contains no cholesterol and no vitamin B12. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: confirmed zero for raw leafy green per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is 0.0 g: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy greens in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for garland chrysanthemum was found. 4. FoodEx2 code A00LC assigned based on Glebionis coronaria raw leafy vegetable classification; FoodOn URI assigned based on FOODON garland chrysanthemum food concept. 5. No SNOMED CT code identified for this food item. 6. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been retained as a classification note given the very low net carbohydrate content (3.02 g carbs − 3.0 g fibre = 0.02 g net carbs per 100 g).
